cards.front: What is a halt in C++?
cards.back: A statement or function that terminates the program immediately, before it would normally end.

cards.text: The two main functions for halting a program are {{c1::std::exit()}} and {{c2::std::abort()}}.
To use std::exit(), include {{c1::<cstdlib>}} header and call {{c2::std::exit(status)}} where status is an integer.

cards.back: It terminates the program immediately with normal cleanup (destructors called for static/global objects), returning a status code to the operating system.

cards.text: By convention, status code {{c1::0}} (or {{c2::EXIT_SUCCESS}}) means success, and non-zero (or {{c3::EXIT_FAILURE}}) means failure.

cards.back: It terminates the program abnormally and immediately, without cleanup (no destructors called, no resources released).

cards.front: What is the key difference between std::exit() and std::abort()?
cards.back: std::exit() performs cleanup (calls destructors) before terminating. std::abort() terminates immediately without any cleanup.

cards.front: Does std::exit() call destructors for local variables?
cards.back: No, only destructors for static and global objects are called. Local variables in the current scope are not destroyed.

cards.explanation:
std::exit() should be used sparingly:
  • Generally avoid in favor of returning from main()
  • May be useful in utility functions that detect fatal errors
  • Appropriate when normal program flow cannot continue
  • Be aware it skips local destructors (resource leaks possible)
  • Prefer exceptions for error handling in most cases
examples:
// Better: return from main
int main() {
    if (error) return 1;
}

cards.back: Almost never. It's mainly used for abnormal termination in catastrophic situations or by assert macros. The lack of cleanup makes it dangerous.

cards.text: The constants EXIT_SUCCESS and EXIT_FAILURE are defined in the {{c1::<cstdlib>}} header.

cards.explanation:
Follow these guidelines:
  • Preferred: Return from main() - cleanest, all destructors called
  • Acceptable: std::exit() when normal flow impossible - but beware local destructors aren't called
  • Avoid: std::abort() - only for catastrophic failures
  • Use exceptions for error handling instead of halts
  • Always clean up resources properly before terminating
examples:
// Best: return from main
int main() {
    if (error) return EXIT_FAILURE;
    return EXIT_SUCCESS;
}
